entities.name: samjñā
entities.cite: the samjna before its connection to the samjnin

axioms.name: rupa_padarthika
axioms.cite: the samjna before its connection to the samjnin is rupa-padarthika — its meaning is just the form

axioms.name: swatantra
axioms.cite: the name has a standing of its own prior to the assignment

axioms.name: nimitta
axioms.cite: Both constructions are licensed, nimittatvaya kalpate, because the name has a standing of its own prior to the assignment

axioms.name: sambandha
axioms.cite: the definitional act then relates two items each with its own claim to case

denials.name: liyate
denials.cite: designation is a relation between two reals, not an absorption of one into the other

claims.name: rupa_padarthika
claims.cite: the name has the form itself as its meaning

claims.name: nimitta
claims.cite: it becomes fit to be the occasion for both the sixth case and the first

rejected_readings.label: absorption_identity
rejected_readings.rendering: The name is ultimately identical with and absorbed into the named thing.
rejected_readings.why: Violates the verse's explicit teaching that samjna and samjnin are distinct reals in relation, not a case of identity or absorption. The designation is a relation (sambandha) between two pre-existing entities, each with its own ontological standing.

rejected_readings.label: dependent_meaning
rejected_readings.rendering: The name has no meaningful reference except through its connection to the named thing.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the axiom that samjna is rupa-padarthika before connection. The form (rupa) provides the name's meaning-object independently of assignment to the samjnin. Without this prior standing (swatantra), the name could not function as nimitta for dual grammatical cases.

notes: The verse articulates a realist semantics for grammatical designation: the samjna has independent ontological standing (rupa-padarthika, 'form as meaning-object') prior to its assignment to the samjnin. This independent status explains why grammar can license both nominative expressions (treating the name as self-standing: 'vrddhi is a-ai-au') and genitive expressions (treating the name as referring: 'of these, the name is vrddhi'). The relation (sambandha) between samjna and samjnin is one between two pre-existing reals, not an absorption of one into the other.
